Triggers.value: Night-time or waking up in the dark

Threat.value: I scan for exits as soon as I enter any room or building
I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat
I feel on guard even inside my own home

Memory.value: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
I can talk about what happened very easily.
The memory is so extreme it is hard to believe it actually happened to me

Negative Appraisals.value: I feel isolated and set apart from others
I am permanently an outsider now

vignette:
Ahmed is a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, married and still working, who describes his life as tightly organized around a terrorist bombing he witnessed years ago. He had been carrying tools into a building when a sudden explosion struck nearby, throwing people to the ground, filling the air with dust and shouting, and leaving him unable to tell where the danger was coming from or what would happen next. He now says that night-time or waking in the dark, bang or explosion sounds, and moments when he feels little control are the situations that disturb him most.

Ahmed describes the traumatic event in brief, separate pictures rather than one continuous scene: the flash, a person on the ground, his own hands clenched around his tools, then another sharp fragment. He can talk about these moments very easily, but he also says the memory is “so extreme it feels unreal,” as though he cannot quite believe it happened to him. When he speaks, he moves from one image to the next without any sense of a full, settled story.

His central belief is that people cannot be trusted, that he is isolated and set apart, and that he is now “permanently an outsider.” He says he no longer feels like someone who belongs with others, even when he is with his wife or among other men at work.

At home, Ahmed reports feeling on guard even inside his own house. When he wakes in the dark, he lies still and listens for any bang outside. In shops, clinics, and customers’ homes, he scans for exits as soon as he enters, and he studies people’s faces and body language for signs that they may be a threat. At work as a plumber, this shows itself in the way he pauses at the doorway of an unfamiliar house, looking first at doors, windows, and who is in the room before he starts the job.

The same themes are present across his account: the bombing memory remains close to the surface, and the present-day situations that matter most to him are night-time, loud sudden sounds, and any sense of lacking control. He speaks of trust in other people with marked suspicion and of feeling permanently separate from those around him. In his daily work, he is careful and watchful in clients’ homes, and his wife says he is harder to settle and less relaxed than he used to be.

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Ahmed is a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, married and still working, but he describes himself as constantly “on edge” since a terrorist bombing he witnessed years ago. He had been near the blast site during the daytime, carrying tools into a building, when a sudden explosion threw people to the ground and filled the air with dust, smoke, and shouting. In that peak moment, he remembers being unable to see where the danger was coming from, with no clear sense of control over what would happen next. Since then, the sound of a bang or explosion, waking in the dark, and any situation that leaves him without control can bring the whole event sharply back.

Ahmed talks about the memory as if it comes in separate pictures: the flash, a body on the pavement, his own hands gripping his tools, then the next scene. He can describe the sequence easily and in detail, yet he also says it feels “too extreme to be real,” as though he is recounting something that happened to someone else. The images are not vague; they arrive as brief, hard-edged moments that he can point to and name.

His central belief is that people cannot be trusted and that he is now “permanently an outsider.” He says he feels isolated and set apart from others, even among family, as if the bombing showed him that the world is divided into those who are safe and those who are not. In his own words, “You never know who is dangerous.”

In everyday life, Ahmed scans exits as soon as he enters a room or building. He studies people’s faces and body language, looking for signs of threat, and even at home he remains on guard. At night, waking in the dark is especially difficult; he lies still, listening, and his attention immediately goes to any sharp sound outside. At work as a plumber, this is particularly disruptive in customers’ houses and crowded building sites, where he repeatedly checks doors and pathways before starting a job.

The dominant cycle is clear. Night-time, bang-like noises, and moments of feeling out of control trigger the memory and intensify his sense of danger. That heightened threat then drives more scanning and guarded behaviour, which keeps the event mentally active and brings the same images and beliefs back again. The more Ahmed checks faces, exits, and the safety of each room, the more he is left feeling separate from others and convinced that trust is dangerous.

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly describes scanning, checking, and guarded behavior as responses driven by triggers, which is explicit causal language linking triggers to a maladaptive strategy.
violations.quote: “Night-time, bang-like noises, and moments of feeling out of control trigger the memory and intensify his sense of danger. That heightened threat then drives more scanning and guarded behaviour...”

violations.quote: “...which keeps the event mentally active and brings the same images and beliefs back again.”

violations.explanation: The vignette explicitly says the scanning and guarded behaviour keeps the event mentally active and brings the images back again, which is a direct causal link from Maladaptive Strategies to Memory.
violations.quote: “...which keeps the event mentally active and brings the same images and beliefs back again.”

vignette_attempts.vignette:
Ahmed is a 58-year-old South Asian plumber, married and still working, who says his life has narrowed since he witnessed a terrorist bombing years ago. He had been carrying tools into a building when a sudden explosion struck nearby, throwing people down, filling the air with dust and shouting, and leaving him unable to tell where the danger was coming from or what would happen next. He now names three situations that are especially difficult: night-time or waking in the dark, bang or explosion sounds, and any moment when he feels he has little control.

Ahmed describes the traumatic event in brief, separate pictures rather than one continuous scene: the flash, a person on the ground, his own hands tight around his tools, then another sharp fragment. He can talk about these moments easily, but he also says the memory is “so extreme it feels unreal,” as if he cannot quite believe it happened to him. The scenes come back as distinct images rather than a smooth story, and he speaks about them with the flat certainty of someone who has rehearsed them many times.

His central belief is that people cannot be trusted, that he is isolated and set apart, and that he is now “permanently an outsider.” He says he no longer feels like someone who belongs with others, even when he is with his wife or among other men at work.

At home, Ahmed reports feeling on guard even inside his own house. When he wakes in the dark, he lies still and listens for any bang outside. In shops, clinics, and customers’ homes, he scans for exits as soon as he enters, and he studies people’s faces and body language for signs that they may be a threat. At work as a plumber, this shows itself in the way he pauses at the doorway of an unfamiliar house, looking first at doors, windows, and who is in the room before he starts the job.

The picture that emerges is one of repeated contact between his present environment and the bombing memory. Night-time, bang-like sounds, and moments of lacking control are all situations Ahmed now notices sharply. The memory is readily available and appears in those isolated fragments, and his sense that people cannot be trusted sits close to his vigilance in rooms, houses, and public places. His checking of exits and faces is a visible part of his daily routine, including in the homes where he works, and his wife says he is harder to settle and less relaxed than he used to be.
